topic: HL Essay: Reflexive Engagement with Anthropology
subject: sociology

items.q: What does 'positionality' refer to in anthropological writing?
items.correct: How the researcher's identity and background shape their access and interpretations.
items.misconception: The physical location where fieldwork takes place.
items.why: Students take 'position' literally as place rather than as the social standpoint of the researcher.

items.q: Why is 'representation' a concern in ethnography?
items.correct: Because the author holds power over how another culture is portrayed.
items.misconception: Because ethnographies must always include photographs.
items.why: Learners overlook the politics of writing about others, reducing representation to mere illustration.

items.q: Does reflexivity make an ethnographic account fully objective?
items.correct: No; it openly acknowledges the researcher's influence rather than eliminating it.
items.misconception: Yes; a reflexive account is completely unbiased.
items.random_wrong: Reflexivity only applies to laboratory science.
items.why: Students assume admitting bias removes it, misunderstanding the purpose of reflexive writing.

items.correct: How the researcher's background shapes their research
items.why: The answer examiners want is: How the researcher's background shapes their research. “The GPS location of fieldwork” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Examine their own role in producing knowledge
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Examine their own role in producing knowledge. “Avoid all fieldwork entirely” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Concerns about 'representation' in ethnography focus on:
items.correct: The power held in portraying another culture
items.misconception: The price of publishing a book
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The power held in portraying another culture. “The price of publishing a book” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Critically engaging with an anthropological argument involves:
items.why: It's Questioning its assumptions and evidence. “Memorising it without analysis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
